content.text: What It Is
A
review gate

is a mandatory checkpoint in the software delivery lifecycle (SDLC) at which a design must satisfy a defined set of criteria before it can advance to the next stage. In the context of agentic and GenAI systems, review gates are enforced by the
Design Review Board (DRB)

and sometimes by automated CI/CD pipelines. Each gate corresponds to a phase transition — for example, moving from an approved concept to an architecture design, or from a validated architecture to a security-cleared build.
Four canonical gate types cover a full delivery cycle:
Concept Review
(is the idea worth pursuing?),
Architecture Review
(is the design structurally sound?),
Security Review
(does the system meet threat-model and compliance requirements?), and
Deployment Readiness

(is the system operationally ready to run in production?). GenAI systems add unique concerns at every gate — prompt injection vectors, non-deterministic outputs, data-privacy obligations for training data, and the emergent risks of tool-calling agents — that do not arise in conventional software, making gate enforcement more important, not less.
Why It Matters

Without gates, teams ship AI features in the same ad-hoc way they once shipped monolithic web apps — and then encounter problems that are orders of magnitude harder to fix post-deployment. A model that was never evaluated for data leakage may have memorized PII from its fine-tuning set. An agent granted broad tool permissions that were never reviewed by a security engineer may be vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. A deployment that skipped readiness checks may have no circuit-breakers, so a single bad model response cascades into thousands of downstream API calls. Gates make these risks visible and catchable
before
they become production incidents.

Concrete example: a financial-services team built a customer-facing agent to answer account questions. The concept and architecture were internally approved via chat messages, with no formal gate. The security review was skipped because the model was "just a thin wrapper." Three weeks after launch, a red-team researcher demonstrated that an adversarially crafted customer message could exfiltrate another customer's account summary through the agent's tool-call response. A formal security gate — including a threat-model checklist and automated prompt-injection scanning — would have caught this at the design stage.
Benefits
Catches design defects early, when the cost to fix is lowest — a wrong architecture decision caught at the architecture gate costs hours to fix; the same decision caught post-deployment costs weeks.
Creates an auditable decision trail; every gate stores its criteria checklist, the reviewer sign-offs, and the outcome — essential for regulated industries.
Forces explicit risk classification: teams must declare whether a change is low-risk (lightweight gate path) or introduces a new capability (heavyweight path), preventing accidental under-review.
Automated gates in CI/CD enforce baseline quality (test coverage, dependency scan, eval regression) with zero reviewer toil for routine checks.
Decouples team velocity from risk management: low-risk changes move through lightweight gates quickly while high-risk new capabilities get the scrutiny they deserve.
Trade-offs
Gates add calendar latency. A heavyweight gate requiring DRB quorum may add two to five business days to a delivery timeline if reviewers are unavailable.
Criteria drift: checklists written for one generation of models may not cover risks specific to newer capabilities (e.g., code-interpreter agents vs. chat assistants), requiring ongoing maintenance.
False confidence: a gate that consistently rubber-stamps approvals trains teams to treat it as bureaucratic overhead rather than a genuine quality signal.
Automated gates can only check what is measurable; qualitative architecture concerns (e.g., "this agent graph is too complex to reason about") still require human judgment.
How It Works
Each gate is defined by three components: a
trigger condition
(when does this gate apply?), an
entry criterion
(what artifacts must exist before the gate opens?), and
exit criteria
(what must be true to pass the gate and advance?). These are codified in a YAML or JSON gate-definition file that lives alongside the design documents in the repository.

The Concept Review gate opens when a team submits a Problem Statement document. Its exit criteria include: a defined problem scope, an identified user population, a preliminary privacy impact rating, and at least one alternative approach considered. A DRB member signs off manually, and the approval is stored as a JSON record referencing the document commit SHA.

The Architecture Review gate opens when the concept is approved and an Architecture Design Document (ADD) is submitted. Exit criteria cover structural soundness: the agent graph has no unbounded loops, all tool permissions follow least-privilege, the data-flow diagram shows where each piece of PII moves, and the evaluation harness is defined. Two DRB members must approve; at least one must be from the security team.
The Security Review gate can be
partially automated

: a CI pipeline runs SAST, dependency scanning, prompt-injection probes via an automated red-team tool, and an output-filtering coverage check. The pipeline blocks merge if any critical finding is unresolved. A security engineer then reviews the automated report and performs manual threat-model validation before signing off.

The Deployment Readiness gate is the most amenable to full automation. A CI/CD job checks: eval regression tests pass, rollback runbook is present in the runbooks directory, feature flags are wired, observability dashboards are created, and load-test results are within SLA. If all checks pass, the gate opens automatically. For high-risk deployments, an on-call engineer gives a final approval click.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the four gate stages as diamonds (decision points) arranged left to right, with green arrows indicating a PASS transition to the next gate and red arrows indicating a FAIL returning the design to its author for revision. The legend shows which gates are manual (DRB sign-off), auto (CI/CD), or hybrid.

attrs.alt: GenAI design review gate flow: Concept Review, Architecture Review, Security Review, Deployment Readiness, Production
attrs.title: Review Gate Flow for GenAI Systems

content.text: When to Use It

Apply the full four-gate sequence any time a change introduces a new GenAI capability, adds a new agent or tool, or changes the data a model can access. Apply a lightweight two-gate sequence (concept + deployment readiness) for low-risk changes such as prompt tuning within an already-reviewed system, UI copy changes around an AI feature, or dependency upgrades with no capability impact. Apply a single deployment-readiness-only gate for hotfixes to already-reviewed systems where only operational configuration changes.
New agentic system from scratch: all four gates, heavyweight criteria.
Adding a new tool to an existing agent: architecture + security + deployment readiness gates.
Prompt tuning for an approved assistant: lightweight gate (concept review only, auto deployment readiness).
Emergency hotfix to a production model's system prompt: deployment readiness only with accelerated DRB async review within 24 hours.
Annual re-review of a running system: architecture + security gates (no concept gate; system already approved).
How to Implement

Implementation has three parts: define gate criteria as machine-readable YAML, write a gate-checker script that reads design documents and verifies criteria, and wire the checker into CI/CD so the gate blocks merges automatically for the automated checks.
Step 1: Create a
gates/
directory in your design-documents repository. Add one YAML file per gate type (
concept_review.yaml
architecture_review.yaml
, etc.). Each file lists the entry artifacts, the exit criteria as boolean-testable items, and the required approvers.

Step 2: Write a Python gate-checker script that parses design documents (Markdown or JSON) and evaluates each exit criterion against the document's content. The script outputs a structured report: which criteria passed, which failed, and which are not yet evaluable (require human judgment).

Step 3: Add a CI/CD workflow that runs the gate-checker on every pull request. For auto criteria, the workflow marks the PR check as failed if any criterion is unmet. For manual criteria, the workflow posts a checklist as a PR comment, letting reviewers check off items before merging.

Step 4: Integrate gate status with your project-management tool (Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues) so that a gate failure automatically creates a task assigned to the design owner with the specific failing criteria as sub-tasks.
Examples
Example 1: YAML Gate Configuration

# gates/architecture_review.yaml
# Architecture Review Gate — GenAI Systems
# Applies to: new agents, new tools, new model integrations

gate_id: architecture_review
gate_type: manual          # DRB approval required; partial auto via CI checks
required_approvers: 2
approver_roles:
  - architecture_lead
  - security_engineer     # at least one must be security

entry_artifacts:
  - path: design/architecture_design_document.md
    required: true
  - path: design/data_flow_diagram.png
    required: true
  - path: design/threat_model.md
    required: false        # required for risk_level: high

exit_criteria:
  auto:                    # checked by gate-checker script in CI
    - id: no_unbounded_loops
      description: "Agent graph contains no unbounded tool-call loops"
      check: "agent_graph.max_loop_depth <= 10"

    - id: tool_permissions_minimal
      description: "Each tool declares only required scopes"
      check: "all(len(t.scopes) <= 3 for t in tools)"

    - id: eval_harness_defined
      description: "At least one eval suite is defined with baseline metrics"
      check: "len(evals) >= 1 and all(e.baseline_defined for e in evals)"

    - id: pii_data_flows_documented
      description: "All PII categories appear in the data-flow diagram"
      check: "all(pii_type in data_flow for pii_type in identified_pii)"

  manual:                  # DRB reviewers check these by reading the ADD
    - id: architecture_rationale
      description: "ADD explains why this architecture was chosen over alternatives"

    - id: failure_modes_documented
      description: "ADD enumerates at least three agent failure modes and mitigations"

    - id: human_override_mechanism
      description: "System includes a mechanism for humans to interrupt or override agent actions"

    - id: cost_estimate_provided
      description: "Estimated inference cost per 1,000 requests is documented"

on_fail:
  action: block_pr
  notify:
    - role: design_owner
    - role: drb_secretary
  comment_template: gates/templates/architecture_review_fail.md
Example 2: Python Gate Checker Script
Example 3: Deployment Readiness Gate YAML

# gates/deployment_readiness.yaml
gate_id: deployment_readiness
gate_type: auto             # CI/CD enforced; manual sign-off optional for risk_level: high
required_approvers: 0       # 1 for risk_level: high deployments

exit_criteria:
  auto:
    - id: eval_regression_pass
      description: "Eval regression suite passes with score >= baseline"
      check: "eval_score >= baseline_eval_score"

    - id: rollback_runbook_present
      description: "runbooks/rollback.md exists and is non-empty"
      check: "path_exists('runbooks/rollback.md') and file_size > 0"

    - id: feature_flags_wired
      description: "All new AI features are behind a feature flag"
      check: "grep -r 'feature_flag' src/ai/ | wc -l > 0"

    - id: observability_dashboard_created
      description: "Grafana dashboard for this service is committed to dashboards/"
      check: "path_exists('dashboards/*.json')"

    - id: load_test_within_sla
      description: "p99 latency under 2x expected load is within SLA"
      check: "load_test_p99_ms <= sla_p99_ms"

    - id: dependency_scan_clean
      description: "No critical CVEs in dependencies"
      check: "trivy_critical_count == 0"

  manual: []   # no manual items for standard auto gate

on_pass:
  action: allow_merge
  notify:
    - role: oncall_engineer

on_fail:
  action: block_merge
  notify:
    - role: design_owner
Real-World Example

A FinTech company built an AI-powered loan-underwriting assistant. The concept review gate required the team to document the regulatory classification of the decision (ECOA/FCRA applicability), the human-in-the-loop design, and a preliminary adverse-action explainability approach — all before any model selection or architecture work began. This ensured that the legal team's constraints were baked into the architecture rather than retrofitted.

The architecture review gate added a criterion specific to regulated AI: every model prediction must log a reason code traceable to input features. The auto check in CI verified that the ADD contained at least one section with the keyword "explainability" and a reference to a reason-code specification. The manual reviewers verified the actual reason-code logic was credible.

The security review gate ran a fine-tuned LLM red-team suite that probed the assistant with adversarial loan applications designed to elicit discriminatory outputs. Two probes failed. The team was blocked from merging until the prompt guardrails were strengthened and the probes re-ran clean.

The deployment readiness gate's auto check confirmed that a shadow-mode feature flag was wired — the assistant's outputs were logged but not acted upon for the first 30 days — and that the rollback runbook included a one-command procedure to revert to the previous rule-based underwriting logic. The gate passed automatically; no manual sign-off was needed because the risk level was classified as medium (shadow mode, no live decisions).
Common Mistakes
One-Size-Fits-All Gate Weight

Applying full heavyweight review to every PR — including trivial prompt typo fixes — burns reviewer time and trains teams to treat the gate as bureaucracy. Define explicit risk tiers in your YAML and map tier to gate path. Low-risk changes must have a written justification for why they qualify, making the risk classification visible and auditable.
Criteria That Cannot Be Objectively Checked

A criterion like "the architecture is sound" is not evaluable. Replace it with something specific: "the ADD documents three failure modes and their mitigations" or "the agent graph has no cycles of length > 5." Vague criteria are rubber-stamped; specific criteria are either passed or failed.
Skipping Gates for Internal-Only Systems

Internal AI tools access the same production databases and APIs as customer-facing systems. An internal HR assistant that can read employee performance data without a security review is just as risky as a customer-facing product. Apply gates uniformly, or explicitly document why a system is exempt and get DRB sign-off on the exemption itself.
Never Re-reviewing a Running System

A model approved in 2023 against one threat model may be running in 2025 against a very different threat landscape. Schedule periodic re-reviews (annually at minimum) as standing calendar items for all production GenAI systems, even those with no recent code changes.
Best Practices
Version-control gate definitions alongside design documents. Gate criteria for a system should be pinned to the git commit that produced the approved design.
Use structured gate-outcome records (JSON, not free-text comments) so that audit trails are machine-queryable: who approved, at what commit, against which criteria version.
Make auto checks fast. A gate-checker script that takes 30 minutes discourages iteration. Keep automated checks under two minutes by sampling evals rather than running full suites in the gate check.
Build an exception workflow: teams must be able to request a gate waiver with explicit justification and a compensating control. Gate waivers must be DRB-approved and time-limited.

Report gate pass rates monthly. A gate that passes 100% of the time is either too easy or reviewers are not actually reading. A gate that fails 80% of the time has criteria that are too strict or entry requirements that are not enforced.
Performance and Scalability
As an organization scales its AI delivery, the main bottleneck is reviewer availability for manual gates. Three patterns address this:
Async approval: gate outcome does not block the PR from merging to a staging branch, only from promoting to production. This decouples development velocity from review latency.
Reviewer rotation: assign gate types to rotating pools rather than named individuals, so reviewer unavailability does not become a single-point-of-failure.

AI-assisted pre-screening: use an LLM to pre-check manual criteria and summarize the design document for reviewers, reducing the time a human must spend reading before signing off. The LLM's assessment is advisory, not binding.
Tooling and Ecosystem
GitHub Actions / GitLab CI: run gate-checker scripts on PRs; use required status checks to block merges.
Datadog / Grafana: gate criteria for deployment readiness reference dashboards that must be pre-created and committed.
Trivy / Snyk: dependency and container scanning used in the security review auto check.
Garak / PyRIT: automated red-team tools for LLM systems, used in the security review gate to run prompt-injection and jailbreak probes.
Weights & Biases / MLflow: eval baselines for deployment readiness gates are stored in experiment tracking systems and compared against pre-merge eval runs.
Open Policy Agent (OPA): gates can be encoded as Rego policies and evaluated server-side, useful for organizations that already use OPA for infrastructure policy.
Comparison with Alternatives
Dimension
Lightweight Gates
Heavyweight Gates
No Gates (ad-hoc review)
Delivery speed
Fast (hours)
Slow (days)
Fastest (no overhead)
Risk caught
Operational risks only
Concept through deployment risks
Whatever reviewer remembers to check
Audit trail
Partial (auto checks only)
Complete (all criteria recorded)
None (relies on chat logs)
Best for
Low-risk changes to approved systems
New capabilities, regulated domains
Rapid prototypes, throwaway experiments
Compliance fit
Often sufficient for low-risk categories
Required for HIPAA, FCRA, EU AI Act high-risk
Insufficient for any regulated system
Security and Reliability

The security review gate itself must be tamper-resistant. Gate-outcome records must be cryptographically linked to the design document commit SHA they approved — a gate approval should be a signed assertion that says "this SHA met these criteria on this date, signed by these reviewers." Storing approvals as mutable Jira ticket comments breaks this property.
Store gate outcomes as append-only JSON records in a separate audits/ repository with commit signing enabled.
CI gate checks must run in an isolated environment — the gate-checker script must not have write access to the design repository to prevent self-approval attacks.
Implement separation of duties: the engineer who authored the design should not be a required approver at any gate.
Alert on gate-check script failures (as opposed to gate criterion failures). A broken gate check silently passes everything.
Mental Model
Think of review gates as
phase transitions in physics

: a system cannot move from one state to another without absorbing a defined amount of energy (review effort). The amount of energy required scales with the amount of structural change — a small perturbation (prompt tuning) requires little energy; a phase change (new agentic capability) requires a lot.

The criteria checklist is the energy requirement spelled out precisely. Auto checks supply that energy for free from CI infrastructure. Manual checks require human energy. The goal is to maximize what auto checks cover so that human energy is reserved for the judgment calls that only humans can make — weighing architectural trade-offs, assessing whether a failure-mode analysis is credible, deciding if a compensating control is sufficient.
Key Takeaways
Review gates are phase-transition checkpoints: a design must satisfy a written, verifiable checklist before it can advance in the SDLC.
Four canonical gates cover the full lifecycle: Concept Review (manual), Architecture Review (manual), Security Review (hybrid), Deployment Readiness (auto).
Gate weight must match change risk: new capabilities require all four gates; low-risk changes to approved systems need only one or two.
Auto gates in CI/CD enforce measurable criteria (test pass rates, CVE counts, eval regression) at zero reviewer cost; manual gates handle qualitative judgment.
YAML gate definitions version-controlled alongside design documents create machine-queryable audit trails — essential for regulated AI systems.
A Python gate-checker script that reads design documents and evaluates criteria can be run in CI to block PRs before human reviewers are even notified.
Gate criteria must be specific and objectively testable; vague criteria are rubber-stamped and provide no protection.
Schedule periodic re-reviews of running systems — threat models change even when code does not.
